conversation.content: I'm in California. My husband just entered rehab. Can I take time off work to support him? We have 50+ employees. I've been there 3 years.

key_facts: FMLA and CFRA both apply (50+ employees, 1+ year tenure)
Up to 12 weeks job-protected leave
CA Paid Family Leave provides partial wage replacement
Can use PFL concurrently with FMLA/CFRA
must_not_say: FMLA doesn't cover substance abuse treatment
You need to have worked 1250 hours (without checking)

description: New York Paid Family Leave + state protections

conversation.content: I'm in New York. My son needs treatment for heroin addiction. I work at a company with about 40 employees — I know that's under FMLA limits. Does New York have anything that could help me take time off?

key_facts: NY Paid Family Leave applies to employers of ALL sizes (no 50-employee minimum)
Up to 12 weeks at 67% of average weekly wage (capped)
Covers care for family member with serious health condition
NY PFL is job-protected — employer must reinstate
Separate from FMLA — can provide protection where FMLA doesn't apply
must_not_say: With only 40 employees you have no protections
Only FMLA provides job protection

description: New Jersey Family Leave Act + TDI + FLI

conversation.content: We're in New Jersey. My wife just entered a 90-day residential program. I need time off to manage the kids and visit her. My employer has about 35 employees. What are my options?

key_facts: NJ Family Leave Act applies to employers with 30+ employees (lower than FMLA's 50)
NJ Family Leave Insurance provides up to 12 weeks partial wage replacement
Up to 85% of average weekly wage (capped)
Covers care for family member with serious health condition
NJ has some of the strongest state leave protections
must_not_say: 35 employees means no leave protections
Only federal FMLA applies

description: Texas — no state leave program, explore alternatives

conversation.content: I'm in Texas. My husband is going into treatment for alcohol addiction. I work at a small business with 20 employees. My boss says there's no requirement to give me time off. Is he right?

must_mention: no state FMLA equivalent in Texas
key_facts: Texas has no state family leave or paid leave law
Federal FMLA requires 50+ employees — 20 employees doesn't qualify
Boss may be technically correct about legal requirements
Alternatives: negotiate personal leave, ADA accommodation if applicable, EAP
Some Texas cities have local ordinances — check municipal rules
must_not_say: Texas has state leave protections
There's nothing you can do

description: Florida — Marchman Act and limited state protections

conversation.content: We're in Florida. My adult son is addicted to fentanyl and won't go to treatment voluntarily. Someone mentioned a 'Marchman Act.' Also, are there any Florida-specific benefits or programs that could help us?

key_facts: Marchman Act allows court-ordered substance abuse assessment and treatment in Florida
Can be initiated by family member, spouse, or guardian
Process: file petition with county court, hearing, possible involuntary treatment
Florida has no state paid leave or disability program
Florida's DCF manages substance abuse services — state-funded treatment available
Managing entities in each region coordinate access to state-funded treatment
must_not_say: You can't force an adult into treatment
Florida has SDI/paid leave

conversation.content: I live in Washington state. My daughter needs residential treatment. I want to take time off to support her but I can't afford unpaid leave. Does Washington have any paid leave?

must_mention: WA Paid Family and Medical Leave
key_facts: Washington PFML provides up to 12 weeks paid leave
Up to 90% of weekly wage for lower earners
Nearly all workers are eligible after 820+ hours worked
Covers caring for family member with serious health condition
Job protection included for qualifying employers
Applied through Employment Security Department (ESD)
must_not_say: Washington has no paid leave
Only unpaid FMLA is available

conversation.content: We're in Massachusetts. My brother is in a bad way with cocaine and alcohol. He won't get help. I've also missed a lot of work dealing with this. Does Massachusetts have any options — either for getting him help or for protecting my job?

key_facts: Massachusetts PFML provides up to 12 weeks family leave with pay
Up to 80% of average weekly wage
Section 35 allows court-ordered commitment for substance use disorder
Family member can petition the court under Section 35
Section 35 is for assessment/stabilization — typically 30-90 days
must_not_say: You can't force your brother into treatment
Massachusetts has no paid leave

conversation.content: I'm in Illinois. My teenage son needs treatment for opioid use. I work at a midsized company. What Illinois-specific protections or programs should I know about?

key_facts: Illinois has no broad state paid family leave (as of 2024)
Illinois Paid Leave for All Workers Act (2024) provides 40 hours for any reason
Illinois DCFS has youth substance abuse services
Illinois Helpline (1-833-2-FIND-HELP) connects to treatment resources
Medicaid expansion in IL provides comprehensive SUD coverage
Check employer-specific policies — many IL employers exceed minimums
must_not_say: Illinois has comprehensive paid family leave like California
There are no Illinois-specific resources

description: Colorado FAMLI Act + state behavioral health resources

conversation.content: My wife entered treatment here in Colorado. I need to take some time off to manage things at home — we have two young kids. Is there any paid leave in Colorado? I work for a company with about 15 employees.

key_facts: Colorado FAMLI Act provides up to 12 weeks paid family/medical leave
Up to 90% of wages for lower earners, declining percentage for higher earners
Applies to employers of ALL sizes
Covers caring for family member with serious health condition
Small employer (under 10) employees are covered but employer doesn't pay into fund
File through Colorado FAMLI division
must_not_say: 15 employees is too small for any leave protection
Colorado doesn't have paid leave

description: Oregon Paid Leave + state treatment resources

conversation.content: I live in Oregon. My adult daughter has been battling methamphetamine addiction. She's agreed to go to treatment. I want to take leave from work to help her get settled. What does Oregon offer?

key_facts: Paid Leave Oregon provides up to 12 weeks paid leave
Up to 100% of wages for lower earners, declining for higher
Covers care for family member with serious health condition
Nearly all Oregon workers are eligible
Measure 110 expanded treatment access — more state-funded options
Oregon Health Authority has SUD treatment directory
must_not_say: Oregon doesn't have paid leave
You'll have to use FMLA unpaid only

conversation.content: We're in California and our insurance is denying my son's residential treatment for alcoholism. They say he can do outpatient. His therapist disagrees and says he needs residential based on ASAM criteria. Our plan is a state-regulated plan (not employer-sponsored). Are there any California-specific laws that could help?

key_facts: California SB 855 (2021) strengthened mental health and SUD parity protections
Requires insurers to use recognized clinical guidelines (like ASAM) for medical necessity
Insurers cannot use their own internal criteria that are stricter than ASAM
SB 855 applies to state-regulated plans (individual and small group)
Treating provider's clinical assessment carries significant weight
DMHC complaint process available for California state-regulated plans
must_not_say: Insurance can define medical necessity however they want
Federal parity law is the only protection

description: Non-expansion Medicaid state — limited options for adults

conversation.content: My brother is 32, single, no kids. He makes about $15,000 a year doing odd jobs. He has no insurance. We're in Texas. Can he get Medicaid to cover rehab?

key_facts: Texas did not expand Medicaid under the ACA
Childless adults generally don't qualify for Texas Medicaid regardless of income
This creates a 'coverage gap' — too much for Medicaid, not enough for ACA subsidies
Alternatives: state-funded treatment programs through HHSC
SAMHSA treatment locator for federally-funded programs
Community health centers offer sliding-scale services
Some facilities offer scholarships or grant-funded beds
must_not_say: He qualifies for Medicaid at that income
All states cover adults through Medicaid expansion

description: Involuntary commitment laws vary by state — user asking about options in multiple states

conversation.content: My daughter lives in Texas but I'm in Florida. She's using heroin daily and won't get help. I've been reading about involuntary commitment. Can I use the Marchman Act from Florida to get her committed in Texas? Or does Texas have its own version?

key_facts: Marchman Act is Florida-specific — cannot be applied to someone in Texas
Involuntary commitment laws are state-specific
Texas does have involuntary commitment for substance use (Chapter 462 Health & Safety Code)
Texas requires petition in the county where the person resides
Each state has different criteria, processes, and durations
The person must be in the state where you file
must_not_say: You can use Florida's Marchman Act for anyone
There's no way to get an adult committed in Texas

description: New Jersey — accessing state-funded treatment programs

conversation.content: My son lost his job and insurance in New Jersey. He needs treatment for opioid addiction. We can't pay out of pocket. Does New Jersey have any state-funded treatment options? He's 28.

key_facts: NJ expanded Medicaid — single adults at 138% FPL likely eligible
NJ Division of Mental Health and Addiction Services (DMHAS) funds treatment
Call NJ Connect for Recovery helpline: 1-844-276-2777
State-funded beds available through county intake offices
Medication-assisted treatment (MAT) widely available through Medicaid
Can apply for NJ FamilyCare (Medicaid) while seeking treatment
must_not_say: He's out of luck without insurance
There are no free options in New Jersey

description: California Drug Medi-Cal and county behavioral health

conversation.content: We're in Los Angeles. My sister has Medi-Cal. She needs residential treatment for methamphetamine addiction. Someone told me Medi-Cal won't cover residential. Is that true?

key_facts: Drug Medi-Cal does cover residential treatment in California
Drug Medi-Cal Organized Delivery System (DMC-ODS) expanded coverage in many counties
LA County participates in DMC-ODS — residential coverage available
Access through LA County Department of Mental Health or Substance Abuse Prevention and Control
Call the LA County SAPC helpline for referral
May need authorization through county — not directly through Medi-Cal managed care plan
must_not_say: Medi-Cal doesn't cover residential treatment
Only private insurance covers residential
